refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address faults before they cause unavailability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability when faults occur.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and inter-node synchronization to eliminate central points of failure and enable partial operation.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDIA) system with automated diagnostics and operator alerting to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP7: Combine redundancy (DP1), modularity (DP2), and monitoring (DP3, DP6) in an integrated availability architecture with defined recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP7: Integrated availability architecture combining (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) modular hot-swappable design for rapid component replacement, (3) predictive condition monitoring to detect degradation early, and (4) comprehensive fault detection and isolation with automated diagnostics—all coordinated to achieve target availability ≥0.97 by minimizing both failure frequency and mean-time-to-repair.

potential_sources: Redundancy strategy (DP1 element) must be coordinated with failover logic timing to avoid cascading failures during switchover.
Modularity boundaries (DP2 element) must align with redundancy domains to ensure swappable units are truly independent.
Condition monitoring (DP3 element) must feed into fault detection (DP6 element) to avoid duplicate sensing and conflicting diagnostics.
Graceful degradation thresholds (DP4 concept, implicit in DP7) must be defined consistently across all subsystems to prevent uncoordinated mode transitions.
Distributed control autonomy (DP5 concept, implicit in DP7) must define synchronization protocol to prevent split-brain scenarios.
coupling_check.notes: DP7 is a composite design that integrates multiple logical strategies. Coupling risk is moderate if boundaries between redundancy domains, modularity interfaces, and monitoring scopes are explicitly defined in the DP-to-IM mapping. Recommend detailed interface specifications and failure mode analysis (FMEA) during IM decomposition.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Target availability ≥0.97 is achievable with the given operational environment (worksite conditions, maintenance access, spare parts availability). Risk: If worksite logistics prevent timely component replacement, MTTR will increase and availability target may not be met.

Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be procured and integrated within cost and schedule constraints. Risk: Redundancy increases system complexity, weight, and cost; customer may not accept trade-off.

Assumption: Predictive maintenance data (condition monitoring signals) are sufficiently reliable to predict failures with low false-positive rate. Risk: Noisy or unreliable sensors may trigger unnecessary maintenance actions, increasing operational disruption.
Assumption: Automatic failover logic is deterministic and testable. Risk: Complex failover scenarios (e.g., partial failures, network partitions) may exhibit emergent behavior not caught in testing.

Assumption: Modular design does not compromise performance or efficiency. Risk: Standardized interfaces and hot-swap capability may impose overhead (e.g., connector losses, protocol latency) that degrades system performance.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies permit automated failover and graceful degradation without manual intervention. Risk: Safety-critical scenarios may require human approval before mode transitions, increasing MTTR.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough to observe representative failure modes and validate availability measurement. Risk: Short evaluation windows may not capture rare failure modes or seasonal variations.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Definition of 'operational availability' (e.g., uptime percentage, mean-time-between-failures, mean-time-to-repair targets), (2) Scope of availability (e.g., does it include planned maintenance windows?), (3) Failure modes in scope (e.g., are environmental faults, operator errors, or supply-chain delays included?), (4) Constraints on redundancy cost/weight/complexity, (5) Maintenance access and spare parts availability assumptions, and (6) Safety-critical constraints that may limit automatic failover. DP7 should then be decomposed into separate DPs for each strategy (redundancy, modularity, monitoring, diagnostics) with explicit coupling rules and interface specifications before IM allocation.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized availability and fault management subsystem responsible for redundancy coordination, hot-swap orchestration, predictive monitoring, fault detection/isolation, and automated diagnostics across all vehicle and teleoperation subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests health, performance, and degradation signals from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to feed predictive condition monitoring and fault detection logic.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits fault isolation results, automated diagnostics, failover events, and availability status to external safety-critical decision makers and operators.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational context and mission constraints; returns availability state and failover readiness to inform task scheduling and resource allocation.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates redundancy state, hot-swap readiness, and diagnostics across distributed Vehicle and Teleoperation nodes for coordinated failover.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: availability, redundancy, fault detection, and diagnostics are inherently safety-critical concerns. Predictive monitoring and hot-swap orchestration align with proactive fault management. Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation must expose health telemetry and accept failover commands, but core DP logic (redundancy strategy, failover logic, condition thresholds, diagnostics rules) resides entirely within Safety Services.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/operational subsystem, not a cross-cutting availability orchestrator. Redundancy coordination, predictive monitoring algorithms, and fault isolation logic would diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution. Hot-swap design is a Vehicle concern, but failover orchestration and diagnostics belong elsewhere.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a control/communication subsystem. Availability architecture spans both Vehicle and Teleoperation; placing it here would fragment redundancy logic and create tight coupling to remote operation semantics. Predictive monitoring and fault isolation would leak into Vehicle and NGHE Solution.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level integration layer. Placing detailed availability logic here would blur boundaries between orchestration and implementation. Redundancy and failover must be tightly coupled to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; centralizing in NGHE Solution creates excessive coupling and diffusion of fault detection across subsystems.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of integrated availability architecture because: (1) availability and fault tolerance are safety-critical concerns; (2) redundancy coordination, failover logic, and diagnostics are cross-cutting and must not be scattered; (3) predictive monitoring and condition thresholds are policy-level decisions best centralized; (4) hot-swap orchestration requires a single authority to manage state transitions; (5) external interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) align with Safety Services' role as a monitoring and decision-making hub.
containment_risks: Risk: Safety Services may become a bottleneck if failover decisions require synchronous communication with Vehicle or Teleoperation. Mitigation: Design asynchronous event-driven failover with pre-computed redundancy policies.

Risk: Predictive monitoring algorithms may require deep domain knowledge of Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. Mitigation: Define clear telemetry contracts and health signal semantics; Safety Services consumes abstract health indicators, not raw subsystem state.

Risk: Hot-swap orchestration may conflict with ongoing operations in Vehicle or Teleoperation. Mitigation: Implement state machines and quiescence protocols to ensure safe component replacement without mission interruption.

Risk: Fault isolation logic may require subsystem-specific diagnostic knowledge. Mitigation: Delegate subsystem-specific diagnostics to Vehicle and Teleoperation; Safety Services aggregates and correlates results.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP7 directly addresses the FR by specifying a comprehensive integrated availability architecture with four concrete mechanisms: (1) redundancy with automatic failover, (2) modular hot-swappable design, (3) predictive condition monitoring, and (4) fault detection/isolation with automated diagnostics. The DP explicitly targets the acceptance criterion (≥0.97 availability) by minimizing both failure frequency and mean-time-to-repair. The mapping is complete and logically sound.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Predictive monitoring algorithms require deep domain knowledge of Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. Mitigation effectiveness depends on clarity of telemetry contracts and health signal abstraction. If subsystem-specific thresholds leak into Safety Services, containment score will degrade.

Moderate risk: Hot-swap orchestration requires synchronization with Vehicle and Teleoperation state machines. Asynchronous event-driven design is specified as mitigation, but implementation must enforce strict separation between Safety Services failover policy and subsystem-specific quiescence protocols.

Low risk: Fault isolation logic may require subsystem-specific diagnostic knowledge. Proposed delegation to Vehicle/Teleoperation with aggregation in Safety Services is sound, but interface contracts must explicitly define diagnostic result semantics to prevent logic diffusion.

Low risk: Safety Services may become a bottleneck if failover decisions require synchronous communication. Pre-computed redundancy policies and event-driven architecture mitigate this, but performance testing must validate latency under high-fault scenarios.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the correct IM owner with 0.92 containment score. The module boundary is well-defined: Safety Services owns redundancy strategy, failover logic, condition thresholds, and diagnostics aggregation. Vehicle and Teleoperation own subsystem-specific health telemetry generation and failover command execution. The 0.08 diffusion score reflects necessary interface coupling (telemetry ingestion, failover command emission) rather than logic diffusion. External interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) are appropriately scoped and unidirectional or bidirectional as needed. No evidence of scattered DP responsibilities across multiple modules.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion (measured availability ≥0.97 over agreed evaluation period) is directly measurable through telemetry aggregation in Safety Services. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to emit health signals to Telemetry interface, (2) logging of failover events and diagnostics via SafetyAlert interface, (3) statistical analysis of uptime over evaluation period. Safety Services' centralized role enables end-to-end availability tracking without diffusion across subsystems.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR allocation to Safety Services IM is unambiguous: Safety Services is responsible for achieving and maintaining the 0.97 availability target through integrated redundancy, monitoring, and fault management. Vehicle and Teleoperation are responsible for exposing health telemetry and executing failover commands. NGHE Solution is responsible for integrating Safety Services with other subsystems and enforcing worksite safety policies. No ambiguity in ownership or responsibility boundaries.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define telemetry contract: Safety Services should specify the abstract health signal schema (e.g., subsystem_id, health_score, degradation_rate, timestamp) that Vehicle and Teleoperation must emit. This prevents subsystem-specific diagnostic logic from leaking into Safety Services.

Formalize failover policy as a declarative configuration: Redundancy strategies, failover thresholds, and hot-swap sequencing should be expressed as policies external to Safety Services core logic. This enables policy updates without code changes and reduces coupling to subsystem specifics.

Define quiescence protocol: Specify the state machine and handshake protocol that Safety Services must follow when orchestrating hot-swap operations. This ensures Vehicle and Teleoperation can safely pause operations and accept component replacement without mission interruption.

Establish diagnostic delegation interface: Define which diagnostic checks are performed by Safety Services (cross-subsystem correlation, availability impact assessment) versus Vehicle/Teleoperation (subsystem-specific health checks). Document the result aggregation and reporting flow.

Add performance acceptance criterion: Specify maximum latency for failover decision and execution (e.g., <100ms from fault detection to failover command emission). This ensures Safety Services bottleneck risk is quantified and testable.

Clarify Network Link interface semantics: Specify whether redundancy state and hot-swap readiness are broadcast asynchronously or queried synchronously. Asynchronous event-driven design is preferred to avoid bottlenecks in distributed failover scenarios.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is logically sound, the IM boundary is well-defined with 0.92 containment, and verification is feasible. However, the recommended_revisions identify critical interface and protocol specifications that are prerequisites for safe decomposition into sub-level FRs. The high safety criticality and moderate diffusion risks (predictive monitoring, hot-swap orchestration) require explicit sub-FR derivation to formalize: (1) telemetry contract abstraction, (2) failover policy formalization, (3) quiescence protocol definition, (4) diagnostic delegation, and (5) performance acceptance criteria. These are not revisions to the current FR-DP-IM but rather sub-problems that must be solved at the next decomposition level to prevent logic diffusion and ensure safety-critical correctness. Proceeding directly to implementation without deriving these sub-FRs would violate axiomatic design discipline and create uncontrolled coupling.
next_level_sub_frs: FR7.1: Establish abstract health signal contract between Safety Services and Vehicle/Teleoperation subsystems such that subsystem-specific diagnostic logic is encapsulated and Safety Services operates on normalized health metrics (subsystem_id, health_score, degradation_rate, timestamp) without knowledge of subsystem internals.

FR7.2: Formalize redundancy and failover policies as declarative, externally-managed configurations (thresholds, strategies, sequencing rules) such that policy updates do not require Safety Services code changes and coupling to subsystem specifics is minimized.

FR7.3: Define quiescence protocol and state machine handshake between Safety Services and Vehicle/Teleoperation such that hot-swap operations can be orchestrated safely without mission interruption and subsystems can pause operations and accept component replacement in a coordinated manner.

FR7.4: Establish diagnostic delegation interface specifying which health checks are performed by Safety Services (cross-subsystem correlation, availability impact assessment) versus Vehicle/Teleoperation (subsystem-specific checks) such that diagnostic logic is not scattered and result aggregation is unambiguous.

FR7.5: Specify failover decision and execution latency acceptance criterion (e.g., <100ms from fault detection to failover command emission) such that Safety Services bottleneck risk is quantified, testable, and performance is validated under high-fault scenarios.

FR7.6: Define Network Link interface semantics for redundancy state and hot-swap readiness communication (asynchronous event-driven vs. synchronous query) such that distributed failover scenarios do not create bottlenecks and state consistency is maintained across subsystems.

assumptions: Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems can emit normalized health telemetry conforming to an abstract schema without exposing subsystem-specific diagnostic logic.
Failover policies can be expressed declaratively and updated externally without requiring Safety Services code changes.
Quiescence protocol can be formalized as a state machine handshake that Vehicle and Teleoperation can implement without violating their own architectural constraints.
Diagnostic delegation can be cleanly separated: Safety Services performs cross-subsystem correlation; Vehicle/Teleoperation perform subsystem-specific checks.
Failover latency can be achieved within <100ms under typical fault scenarios without requiring synchronous communication that creates bottlenecks.
Network Link interface can support asynchronous event-driven communication for redundancy state and hot-swap readiness without introducing race conditions or state inconsistency.
open_risks: Telemetry contract abstraction may fail if Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems cannot normalize their health signals without leaking domain-specific logic. Mitigation: Explicit schema definition and validation at interface boundaries.

Failover policy formalization may be insufficient if real-world fault scenarios require dynamic policy adaptation. Mitigation: Design policies to be composable and support runtime reconfiguration within Safety Services.

Quiescence protocol may introduce unacceptable latency or mission interruption if subsystems cannot pause operations quickly. Mitigation: Performance testing and protocol validation during sub-FR decomposition.

Diagnostic delegation may create ambiguity if subsystem-specific checks produce results that Safety Services cannot interpret. Mitigation: Explicit result semantics definition and aggregation logic formalization.

Failover latency may exceed <100ms if Safety Services becomes a bottleneck under high-fault scenarios. Mitigation: Pre-computed redundancy policies and event-driven architecture validation during implementation.
Network Link interface may introduce state inconsistency if asynchronous communication is not properly synchronized. Mitigation: Formal protocol definition and distributed consensus validation.
evidence_needed: Telemetry schema specification: Define abstract health signal structure (subsystem_id, health_score, degradation_rate, timestamp) and validation rules. Validate that Vehicle and Teleoperation can emit conforming signals without subsystem-specific logic leakage.

Failover policy formalization: Express redundancy strategies, thresholds, and sequencing as declarative policies. Demonstrate that policies can be updated externally and applied by Safety Services without code changes.

Quiescence protocol specification: Define state machine, handshake sequence, and timing constraints. Validate that Vehicle and Teleoperation can implement protocol without violating their own constraints.

Diagnostic delegation interface: Specify which checks are performed by Safety Services vs. subsystems. Define result semantics and aggregation logic. Validate that results can be interpreted unambiguously.
Failover latency measurement: Instrument Safety Services to measure latency from fault detection to failover command emission. Validate that <100ms criterion is achievable under high-fault scenarios.
Network Link interface validation: Specify asynchronous event-driven communication semantics. Validate that state consistency is maintained across subsystems under concurrent failover scenarios.
